exemplar.title: 9. Are some types of knowledge less open to interpretation than others?

detailedExemplarData.title: 9. Are some types of knowledge less open to interpretation than others?

strengths: Provides a clear, organized presentation of information through tables.
Demonstrates some technical competence in listing and formatting Excel shortcuts.
weaknesses: No selection or identification of three distinct real-world objects.
No reference to or justification of an IA prompt, knowledge question, or TOK concept.
Absence of any contextualisation or analytical commentary linking content to TOK.
Entire submission remains descriptive with no engagement in justification or evidence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examinerâs total mark of 5 is definitive. Although the submission does not meet the 1â2 band criteriaâlacking three objects, real-world contexts, a chosen IA prompt, and links to TOK conceptsâthe examinerâs overall mark must be allocated in full. Since there is only one gradable criterion (Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?), the entire 5 points are assigned here. The strengths noted reflect minimal organizational effort, but the critical weaknesses align with a Band 0 performance. Allocating all 5 points to this single criterion satisfies the reverseâgrading requirement that the criterion scores sum exactly to the examinerâs total.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student provides only a set of generic tables listing Microsoft Excel keyboard shortcuts. There is no indication that an official IA prompt has been selected, nor are three distinct real-world objects explicitly identified or contextualised. No attempt is made to explain how any object relates to knowledge questions, to TOK concepts, or to the world beyond the spreadsheet interface.  

Because the work lacks an IA prompt, object commentary, real-world context, and any form of justification or analysis, it fails to reach the minimal standards described in the 1â2 band. Consequently, the appropriate placement is the 0 band, as the exhibition does not meet the basic rubric requirements.

annotations.comment: The descriptive sentence preceding the table does not reference a knowledge question or TOK concept. The student should link this explanation to the selected IA prompt and real-world context.

The entire table lists generic Excel commands without identifying any specific real-world object or context. The student should select three concrete objects and explain their relevance to a TOK prompt.

The title âFrequently used shortcutsâ appears disconnected from a TOK exhibition. There is no indication of an IA prompt or thematic link. The student should clarify the chosen prompt and how the title relates to exploring knowledge in the world.

Including URLs without commentary misses an opportunity to contextualize sources as TOK objects. The student should explain why these web links are significant and how they relate to knowledge questions.
The MAC URL is presented without analysis. To meet TOK criteria, the student should justify how this link constitutes a realâworld object or example relevant to their chosen TOK prompt.
Listing âPage Downâ without commentary is a missed opportunity. The student should explore how this function embodies assumptions about information navigation.
The student continues listing commands such as âMove to the next sheet,â but no TOK rationale is provided. They must justify each choice in terms of knowledge exploration.
Merely stating âCtrl+Page Downâ neglects interpretation. The student should link this shortcut to a real-world object or process that illustrates their TOK prompt.
The final entry âTabâ closes the listing but offers no reflection. The student must move beyond description to analyze how each shortcut shapes knowing.
No three objects or their real-world contexts are present in the entire document. The student must choose and justify three distinct objects linked to a TOK prompt.
The header âTo do thisâ and âPressâ remain purely functional. In a TOK exhibition, students should rename or contextualize table elements to reflect their chosen prompt and objects.
Listing âClose a spreadsheetâ lacks any explanation of its significance for knowledge production. The student should justify why this action matters in their TOK exploration.
The keystroke âCtrl+Wâ is presented without any TOK analysis or link to knowledge questions. The student should avoid mere descriptions and focus on conceptual connections.
Across this page, there is no evidence of a selected IA prompt or three objects. The student should explicitly state the prompt and choose three specific real-world objects as required.
Continued listing of generic commands on page 2 omits any link to TOK concepts. The student should pivot from description to critical analysis of knowledge issues.
The row âGo to Data tabâ is a rote instruction. To develop a TOK exhibition, the student needs to reflect on what this action reveals about knowledge systems or processes.
Presenting âAlt+Aâ without interpretation misses a chance to explore how keyboard shortcuts shape user knowledge. The student should analyze its broader implications.
The second table on page 2 continues descriptive entries, but no objects are identified. The student should choose concrete items and justify their selection.
Even formatting choices like bolding âPressâ lack contextual rationale. In TOK, every stylistic decision should reinforce the argument and prompt connections.
The row describing movement commands is purely functional. The student should transform such descriptions into explorations of how these tools influence our ways of knowing.
The keystroke âShift+Tabâ remains unattributed to any TOK concept. The student needs to integrate each command into a broader analysis of knowledge.
Across page 2 there is still no selection of three objects or real-world contexts. The student must explicitly identify objects and link them to the IA prompt.
The table on page 3 continues listing actions without TOK framing. The student should stop descriptive listing and start contextual discussion of knowledge practices.
The header âPressâ here is mechanistic. The student should rename or frame it to align with their TOK prompt, making its purpose in the analysis explicit.
Describing âMove one screen downâ in isolation fails to show how this reflects broader knowledge issues. The student should link each action to a TOK concept.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition does not reach the standard described by the other levels or does not use one of the IA prompts provided.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of the objects may be implied rather than explicitly stated. Basic links between the objects and the selected IA prompt are made, but the explanation of these links is unconvincing and/or unfocused. There is a superficial justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Reasons for the inclusion of the objects are offered, but these are not supported by appropriate evidence and/or lack relevance to the selected IA prompt. There may be significant repetition across the justifications of the different objects

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of these objects may be vaguely or imprecisely stated. There is some explanation of the links between the three objects and the selected IA prompt. There is some justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Some of the points are supported by evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects and their real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are explained, although this explanation may lack precision and clarity in parts. There is a justification of the contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. Many of the points are supported by appropriate evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition clearly identifies three objects and their specific real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are clearly made and well-explained. There is a strong justification of the particular contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. All, or nearly all, of the points are well-supported by appropriate evidence and explicit references to the selected IA prompt.
